notes: Concentrated in herbal pages. First word on many botanical pages is qok- family. Consistent with plant naming function.
Associated with leaves (shedy), water/pouring (shey), sun/dissolving (shol). Mid-paragraph clustering.
Most frequent prefix family. Broadly distributed. Associated with plant parts (branches, bark, sap) and mixing.
End-of-paragraph concentration. Strongly associated with therapeutic conclusions. otar as 'remedy' marker.
Late-paragraph position. Medical application forms: ointment, balm, lotion.
Beginning-of-paragraph. Root/earth descriptions. cth- gallows character suggests marked category.
Strongest positional signal. >20% line-initial. Every ~19 words in Currier A vs ~50-60 in B. Best candidate for Latin 'item'.
Mixed category. May include prepositions (dal≈de illa), verbs (dar≈dare), and nouns (dol≈dolor).
Rare prefix. Possibly astronomical (star) or temporal reference. Concentrated in specific sections.
Possibly equivalent to pharmaceutical 'Recipe' (Take). Opening instruction marker.
